title: Layout and Navigation Decisions
intro: Your task manager needs a layout structure and navigation system. Similar to an architect's floor plan, the layout defines where everything lives and how users move between sections. The choices you make now determine how the app feels to use on both mobile and desktop. Let's see how you handle the key decisions.

teach.title: Layout Sets the Stage
teach.body: Your app's layout is the skeleton everything hangs on. In Next.js, the root layout wraps every page and is the right place for persistent UI like navigation bars and sidebars. A well-structured layout means pages focus only on their own content, with shared chrome handled once.

teach.title: Tailoring a Suit vs. Letting It Out
teach.body: It's easy to take a well-fitted small suit and let out the seams to make it larger. But shrinking an oversized suit into a slim fit? That means cutting fabric, re-stitching everything, and hoping it doesn't look awkward. Mobile-first design works the same way — start with the small screen and expand gracefully for bigger ones, rather than trying to squeeze a desktop layout into a phone.

teach.title: Mobile-First, Desktop-Enhanced
teach.body: Most people check their tasks on their phone. If you design for desktop first and then try to cram it onto mobile, the experience will feel broken. Start with the mobile layout — a single column, touch-friendly buttons, bottom navigation — and then **enhance** for desktop with a sidebar or wider grid.

teach.title: Layout Is Like a Building's Floor Plan
teach.body: Before a single wall goes up, an architect draws a floor plan showing where the hallways, rooms, and exits go. Your app's layout is the same — it defines where the navigation lives, where content goes, and how users move between sections. A confusing floor plan means people get lost; a confusing layout means users can't find features.

Great navigation is invisible — users just find what they need. Clunky navigation makes people uninstall. Getting layout right means your app feels professional, and professional-feeling apps are the ones people pay for.

Use Next.js layout files to define shared structure, design mobile-first using Tailwind's responsive prefixes, and keep navigation simple. A task manager needs at most 3-4 top-level navigation items. If you need more, your scope is too big.

start.narrative: You're setting up the main layout for your task manager in Next.js. The app has three sections: a task list, a settings page, and a profile page. How do you structure the navigation?

choices.text: Build a fixed sidebar with links to all three sections, visible at all times on every screen size
Start with a bottom tab bar for mobile with icons for each section, then switch to a sidebar on larger screens using responsive classes
Use a hamburger menu on all screen sizes to keep the UI minimal

responsive-nav.narrative: Your bottom tab bar works great on mobile. On desktop, the sidebar shows section names alongside icons. Now you need to decide where to put the 'Add Task' action — the most important action in the entire app. Where does it go?

choices.text: A floating action button (FAB) in the bottom-right corner on mobile, and a prominent 'Add Task' button at the top of the sidebar on desktop
Add it as the center item in the bottom tab bar on mobile
Put the 'Add Task' button only on the task list page, at the top of the list

fixed-sidebar.narrative: On desktop, the sidebar looks fine. But on mobile, the fixed sidebar takes up half the screen width, leaving barely any room for the task list. Users can see only 2-3 tasks at a time. What do you do?

choices.text: Convert the sidebar to a bottom tab bar on mobile using Tailwind responsive classes: 'hidden md:flex' for sidebar, 'flex md:hidden' for bottom bar
Make the sidebar collapsible with a toggle button

hamburger.narrative: The hamburger menu hides all navigation behind a tap. On mobile, users have to tap the hamburger, wait for the menu to animate open, find their section, and tap again — 3 actions to navigate. Analytics show users rarely visit Settings or Profile because the navigation is hidden.

choices.text: Replace the hamburger with a visible bottom tab bar on mobile so navigation is always one tap away
Keep the hamburger but make it bigger and add a tooltip saying 'Menu'

deep-linking.narrative: A user sends you a screenshot of a task asking for help — but when you click the link they copied, the app opens the task list, not the specific task. How do you handle routing?

choices.text: Use Next.js dynamic routes (/tasks/[id]) so every task has its own shareable URL
Open the task in a modal and put the ID in a query param (?taskId=123) so refreshing keeps the modal open
Keep all tasks under one route — users can search for it when they enter the app

loading-states.narrative: When users navigate between sections, the screen goes blank while data loads. On slow connections it feels like the app froze. How do you handle loading states?

choices.text: Use Next.js Suspense with skeletons that match the final layout — users see structure while data arrives
Show a centered spinner while each page loads
Leave the screen blank — users understand it's loading

keyboard-nav.narrative: A keyboard-only user reports that after switching sections, focus is lost and they have to press Tab several times to reach the content. How do you fix keyboard navigation?

choices.text: Move focus to the main heading on each route change, and ensure tab order follows the visual flow
Add a 'Skip to content' link that appears on first Tab
Most users use a mouse — it's not a priority

excellent-end.narrative: Your app feels native on every device. Navigation is responsive, the primary action is always reachable, URLs are shareable, loading states show structure, and the keyboard works the way it should. Users don't think about navigation — they just use it.
excellent-end.feedback: Flawless execution. You solved five UX problems many apps ignore: mobile-first responsiveness, primary action placement, deep linking, skeleton loading states, and keyboard focus management. This is what separates an app that 'looks pretty' from one that 'feels professional.'

good-end.narrative: Your app is functional and most users are happy. The skip-link helps keyboard users, though some still get confused when focus doesn't move automatically when they change pages.
good-end.feedback: Solid result. The skip-link is a good baseline, but proactive focus management is what makes the app feel truly accessible — not just legally compliant. Consider adding focus management on route transitions as the next step.

broken-end.narrative: Your app looks great for mouse users, but analytics show high abandonment in sessions detected as 'keyboard navigation'. An accessibility audit flags it as non-conforming to WCAG, and now an enterprise customer can't use it.
broken-end.feedback: Accessibility isn't optional — it's a legal requirement in many contexts and an inclusion decision in all of them. Keyboard users include people with mobility limitations, power users, and new users who haven't mastered the mouse yet. Managing focus doesn't take much code and opens your app to more people.
